opp.ais_why:
• Champion Tharun Chandirasekaran engaged the January 29 2026 demo on agentic capability across multiple dimensions. He pulled for custom AI prompt creation via the Merlin Agentic platform for end-to-end negotiation flows, asked for the rationale behind AI-suggested discount percentages, and requested a backend chemical supplier search via the autonomous agent. AI importance is rated Very Critical.
• Tharun raised concrete agentic-platform questions: surfacing Anna's negotiation rationale in the UI, Outlook integration for auto-syncing contract red-lines, RFX Scoring Pro automating supplier response scoring with AI, and AI-driven supplier identification via supplier.io. These are capability-fit probes, not generic curiosity. The discovery has moved past pitch into evaluation.
• Five completed meetings between December 2025 and March 2026 with Tharun as the consistent champion, plus legal counsel Chris Klein joining for contract module review and Ryan Anderson handling integration. Pricing has been quoted across iContract, iSource, iAnalyze, iRisk and ANA modules. With Very Critical AI posture and an agentic AI champion driving the relationship, this is a high-confidence Hungry deal.

metrics.narrative: The operational pain is well established but never converted to a financial business case. On the 3 Dec 2025 intro call the buyer quantified scale: '$300-400 million in combined direct and indirect spend managed by a central sourcing team of 9-10 people' (key takeaway, Touchpoint 1), and on 29 Jan ~30-35 total users were sized. Tharun made ROI an explicit decision gate on the 26 Feb AI call: when opting for all three modules 'we want to see what is the return total investment and the return on investment, the integration complexity', and on 26 May reinforced 'cost also matters... end of the day'. Despite pricing being shared on 30 Jan, no ROI/savings model (time saved, compliance-risk reduced, maverick spend recaptured) has been delivered, so the value case remains qualitative. Building and presenting that quantified ROI is a ranked move.

sources: Task 19 May 2026 (OOO naming manager Reem Alghanem)
champion.narrative: Tharun Chandirasekaran (Sourcing Analyst, self-described super-user and POC) is an active, engaged champion: he drove all 7 touchpoints, produced the requirements checklist, looped in IT (Ryan Anderson) and legal (Chris Klein, Laura McCarthy), and on 26 May proactively reassured 'it's not like we are pursuing this with a different supplier'. However he is a developing champion, not yet a power champion: he lacks budget authority, cannot close on his own ('I'll be providing my feedback to my VP'), and his access to Jackie/COO has not translated into executive engagement. What has developed: deep technical advocacy and continuous communication. What is missing: an executive sponsor relationship and proof he can move Jackie. Out-of-office auto-reply named Reem Alghanem as Tharun's manager — a potential new thread to develop.

competition.narrative: The dominant competitor is the status quo / do-nothing path: on 26 May the project was paused with 100% focus shifted to cost mitigation, and the buyer runs a mature incumbent stack (D365 ERP, Coupa for onboarding/invoicing, Ariba/Blackstone for e-bidding, Power BI for analytics) that they explicitly will not replace ('we're not getting rid of Coupa'). Coupa is a quasi-competitor for the AI/sourcing scope — on 26 Feb Bikash (Zycus) noted Coupa 'already have the supplier piece... maybe a natural choice', and the buyer is weighing whether to build around Coupa. A two-vendor split is a live alternative: Tharun stated (26 Feb) that if CLM exceeds budget they would 'diversify the project between two different vendors' (one CLM, one sourcing). SAP Ariba is incumbent for e-bidding but not actively being expanded. Zycus wins on the integrated suite + agentic AI (Merlin/Anna) story and the CLM gap no incumbent fills; the wedge is proving ROI to beat do-nothing and avoid the vendor split.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is specific, multi-sourced and owned by the sourcing team. On 12 Dec Tharun detailed: no contract management tool at all ('we don't house those contracts in any systems'), contracts tracked manually via Word and an online repository (ContractWorks) that is 'pretty primitive... not much insight', renewals/utilization tracked only through custom Power BI, and indirect spend (new since July 2025 after 20-30 acquisitions) is 'a lot fragmented' with no approved vendor list. Reverse auctions are outsourced to Blackstone via email/Excel. The 11 Mar CLM demo confirmed the pain owner's words: negotiation done by 'downloading/uploading Word documents causing version-control delays'. The pain owner is Tharun (sourcing analyst/super-user) with legal (Laura, Chris) owning the CLM redline pain.

sources: no commercial/contracting evidence in any source
paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics are largely unknown because the deal never reached commercials. No MSA/SOW, legal redline of a Zycus agreement, procurement channel, or InfoSec process has been discussed for the purchase itself (the legal team's involvement to date is as CLM end-users evaluating the tool, not as contract counsel for the Zycus deal). DocuSign is the buyer's e-signature tool (confirmed 11 Mar) which is useful color but not the purchase paper process. What would create visibility: at the Q3 recap, confirm who owns vendor contracting and procurement sign-off, and whether IT/InfoSec review is required before a PO — and map that timeline backward from any target date.

economic_buyer.narrative: The economic buyer is identified but not engaged. Jackie Zhang (VP Procurement) is the named Decision Maker and budget authority; on the 26 Feb call Tharun confirmed the chain: 'VP of procurement... our VP reports to the COO', placing final budget approval with the COO. Jackie attended only the first two calls (3 Dec, 12 Dec 2025) and has not engaged since, despite repeated rep attempts to loop her back in (Next Step 2/17 'loop Jackie VP back into convos', 4/13 'adding meeting w/ Jackie for last week of April' — that meeting did not occur; an outbound dial to Jackie on 19 Mar logged 'Could Not Connect'). The COO has never been engaged. Technical validation is complete but the executive layer that controls budget and timing is absent — securing a Jackie/COO alignment call at the Q3 restart is the single highest-leverage gap.

decision_process.narrative: The process is clearly mapped from the call sequence and stated by the buyer. On 26 Feb Tharun described it: interview ~10 vendors, consolidate to a top-3 shortlist by end of Q1, then introduce the wider team plus the VP and sourcing leaders for group demos, IT engages separately on integration complexity, and the C-suite is approached in parallel for budget. The call sequence corroborates: discovery (Dec) to demos (Jan) to AI deep dive (Feb) to legal CLM deep dive (Mar, Laura McCarthy and Chris Klein joining signals legal-gate entry). Crucially the process stalled: on 26 May Tharun said the company introduced a new internal team with a dedicated lead running an approval workflow, pausing vendor work until Q3 — 'it's kind of going through an approval workflow within the internal chain'. The buyer-owned next milestone is a recap demo for all new stakeholders at Q3 restart.

decision_criteria.narrative: Criteria are explicit and documented. Tharun shared a core/nice-to-have requirements checklist for both CLM and Sourcing (emails 12 Jan and 23 Jan 2026) that the demos were tailored against. The decisive criteria, repeated across calls: (1) Contract Lifecycle Management as top priority (clause libraries, risk profiling, automated redlining, dual supplier/customer paper), (2) bidirectional integration with D365, Coupa and Power BI ('integration capability is critical... must sync', 12 Dec key takeaway), (3) AI/agentic value, and (4) total cost and ROI as gating factors. Legal added ease-of-use and feature relevance as equal-weight CLM criteria (26 Feb). On 11 Mar the legal team asked specific functional questions (force majeure extraction, version control, audit trail) showing active criteria validation.

items.detail: No formal ROI model or savings quantification has been delivered. Buyer confirmed ROI is decision gate (1/29 call Tharun: 'decision depends on total/return on investment'; 5/26 call: 'cost also matters... end of the day'). Pricing shared 1/30, but no ROI workshop or financial business case provided. Buyer is evaluating phased approach (CLM first, then sourcing, then AI if budget allows per 1/29 call), signaling budget constraint. Business case is qualitative (confirmed operational pain: $300–400M spend, 9–10 FTEs, manual processes, no contract alerts) but not financial. Vulnerability: buyer may defer full-suite investment if ROI is not proven, retaining the Coupa+Ariba+custom-Power-BI status quo.

D365/Coupa bidirectional integration complexity is a stated decision criterion and an unquantified risk. Discovery 12/12: 'integration capability is critical... must sync with D365, Coupa, Power BI dashboards to maintain current reporting workflows.' IT lead Ryan Anderson attended three calls (12/12, 1/14, 1/29) for integration questions, but no formal IT scoping outcome or integration cost estimate has been delivered. Tharun on 2/26 stated plan to 'engage IT to assess integration complexity... before finalizing AI scope and budget,' but this has not occurred. Buyer's concern: if D365/Coupa sync is too complex or costly, it undermines the ROI and could kill the deal. Integration scoping is the second gate (alongside ROI) blocking commercial negotiation. Risk: if integration cost estimate comes in high or IT flags technical complexity, buyer may defer investment or seek simpler alternative.

Legal team (Chris Klein, Laura) holds veto power on CLM vendor with 'equal weightage' in the decision (Tharun on 2/26 call), but no formal legal sign-off or scorecard has been provided post-3/11 CLM demo. Legal engaged positively (Laura requested sandbox, Chris validated workflows), but their true satisfaction level and internal recommendation are unconfirmed. Vulnerability: if legal prefers a competitor or finds Zycus CLM too complex, they can block the deal despite strong Tharun support.

Geopolitical cost-mitigation priorities (Iran tariffs, price increases) drove 100% of sourcing team focus from April through May 2026, pausing the project until Q3 (5/26 call: 'our focus went 100% into cost mitigation and supply negotiations'). This external forcing function is outside Zycus's control and affected all departments company-wide, not sourcing alone. Risk: if geopolitical conditions worsen or similar external shocks occur, buyer's attention and budget may be diverted again, further delaying timeline. Buyer confirmed on 5/26 that the pause was a business decision 'not made by sourcing but implemented throughout the company,' signaling vulnerability to macroeconomic/political events. Current state: geopolitical pause lifted, Q3 restart confirmed, but recurrence risk remains.

best_practice_check.summary: Three win-strategy levers for the Q3 restart: convert the strong technical evaluation into executive sponsorship, replace inertia with a quantified ROI, and protect the suite from a two-vendor split.

competitive_position.summary: The strongest current threat is the status quo / do-nothing path: as of 26 May 2026 the project is paused with 100% focus on cost mitigation, and the buyer retains a mature Coupa + Ariba + D365 + Power BI stack they will not replace. Coupa is the medium live threat for the AI/sourcing scope (Zycus's own consultant flagged it as the 'natural choice'). A two-vendor split (CLM to one vendor, sourcing to another) is a live structural risk if CLM exceeds budget. SAP Ariba is a low-threat incumbent confined to e-bidding. No third-party suite competitor (e.g. a rival CLM vendor) has been named on any call.

competitors.name: Coupa
competitors.quote: I would say is maybe a natural choice if I had been in your place would be to go for Coupa than looking for a third party solution
competitors.how_we_win: Position iSupplier/iContract integrating with Coupa (not replacing it) and show Coupa cannot match the CLM + agentic AI (Merlin/Anna) depth.

competitors.name: SAP Ariba
competitors.quote: Existing e-bidding via Ariba and invoice payments through Coupa on top of Dynamics 365 ERP
competitors.how_we_win: Ariba is confined to e-bidding via Blackstone; highlight Zycus's integrated S2P + CLM coverage Ariba does not provide here.

competitors.name: Do-Nothing / Status Quo
competitors.quote: We don't have a tool which tracks, okay, a contract is about to expire on a certain date or pushes any alert messages or a tool which tracks how much we have spent right now associated to a certain contract. We're kind of like extracting the reports from the system and linking it to the contracts through the Power BI platform, which is customized by our internal team. When it comes to indirect, we don't have any approved vendor list or something like that. We're going ahead and manually tracking that via email-based RFQs.
competitors.how_we_win: Quantify the cost of the status quo (9–10 FTEs managing $300–400M spend with manual email RFQs, custom Power BI reports, no contract alerts, no spend-utilization tracking) and prove Zycus delivers measurable time savings, compliance-risk reduction, and savings capture that pays for itself in <18 months. Make inaction the riskiest option by tying the pain to dollars and headcount.

competitors.name: Status Quo / Do-Nothing
competitors.quote: our focus kind of went 100% into cost mitigation and supply negotiations... the project was pushed to Q3
competitors.how_we_win: Deliver a quantified ROI model (savings, compliance risk, maverick spend) so the suite beats inertia, and re-anchor on the CLM gap no incumbent fills.

competitors.name: Two-vendor split (CLM and sourcing to different vendors)
competitors.quote: we would think about diversifying the project between two different vendors. One would be taking the CLM, the other would be taking the sourcing
competitors.how_we_win: Prove suite synergy and single-vendor TCO advantage; price CLM within budget to keep both modules with Zycus.

hard.ais_why:
• Champion Tharun Chandirasekaran engaged the January 29 2026 demo on agentic capability across multiple dimensions. He pulled for custom AI prompt creation via the Merlin Agentic platform for end-to-end negotiation flows, asked for the rationale behind AI-suggested discount percentages, and requested a backend chemical supplier search via the autonomous agent. AI importance is rated Very Critical.
• Tharun raised concrete agentic-platform questions: surfacing Anna's negotiation rationale in the UI, Outlook integration for auto-syncing contract red-lines, RFX Scoring Pro automating supplier response scoring with AI, and AI-driven supplier identification via supplier.io. These are capability-fit probes, not generic curiosity. The discovery has moved past pitch into evaluation.
• Five completed meetings between December 2025 and March 2026 with Tharun as the consistent champion, plus legal counsel Chris Klein joining for contract module review and Ryan Anderson handling integration. Pricing has been quoted across iContract, iSource, iAnalyze, iRisk and ANA modules. With Very Critical AI posture and an agentic AI champion driving the relationship, this is a high-confidence Hungry deal.

deltas.subject: Quantify the ROI to overcome status-quo inertia and budget constraint—Buyer confirmed ROI is a decision gate (Tharun on 1/29: 'decision depends on total/return on investment'; on 5/26: 'cost also matters... end of the day'), and pricing was shared on 1/30, but no ROI workshop or savings quantification has been delivered. Buyer is evaluating a phased approach (CLM first, sourcing second, AI third if budget allows per 1/29 call) and on 5/26 stated 'if everything falls into place, we'll opt for all features. If not, we'll eliminate AI first. If CLM doesn't meet requirements or goes beyond budget, we'll diversify to two vendors.' This signals budget constraint and risk of deferring the full-suite investment if ROI is not proven. Status Quo / Do-Nothing is the primary competitive threat (high threat_level). Next move: deliver ROI workshop in first two weeks of Q3 restart (mid-July 2026) quantifying three impact areas: (1) time saved—contract authoring, negotiation cycles, renewal tracking (estimate 20–30 hours/month FTE savings across 9–10 person team = $50–75K annual labor cost avoided); (2) compliance risk reduced—missed renewals, unapproved spend (estimate 5–10% of $300–400M addressable spend = $15–40M at-risk spend brought under control); (3) maverick spend captured—approved vendor list enforcement for indirect (estimate 3–5% savings on indirect tail spend = $3–6M annual savings). Anchor ROI to buyer's own operational scale ($300–400M spend, 9–10 FTEs) to justify the $115K investment.

Frame it as a pre-commercial gate: 'Before we move to a formal proposal, let's get Ryan's sign-off on the integration approach and cost so there are no surprises.' deltas.subject: Engage Jackie Zhang (VP Procurement, Economic Buyer) and COO (final budget approver) to validate strategic fit and budget authority. Jackie attended first two discovery calls (12/3, 12/12) but has not engaged since January 2026 despite multiple re-engagement attempts (Next_Step 2/17, 3/4, 4/6, 4/13). COO has never been engaged (Tharun 5/26 call: 'VP reporting to COO'). Commercial and pricing handled entirely by Tharun (analyst-level champion), not by EB/COO. Without EB/COO validation, deal cannot advance from technical validation (complete) to commercial negotiation (Proposal/Negotiation stage). Recommended move: (1) Deliver ROI model to Tharun and ask him to validate the business case internally with Jackie and COO. (2) Request Tharun to schedule 30-min executive alignment call with Jackie and/or COO during Q3 restart window (early July 2026) to present ROI narrative, validate budget, confirm timeline, and secure strategic sponsorship. Frame as: 'Jackie and the COO need to hear the business case directly to approve the investment – let's get 30 minutes on their calendar to walk through the ROI and answer any executive questions'. If Tharun cannot secure the call, that signals low executive sponsorship and the deal is at risk. This is the second-highest-leverage move.

deltas.subject: Complete IT D365/Coupa integration scoping to de-risk the technical decision gate. (2) Deliver one-page integration scoping summary with cost estimate to Tharun and Ryan post-call. This converts 'partially aligned' to 'confirmed aligned' on the second stated decision criterion and removes buyer's single technical blocker. This is the third-highest-leverage move.

Close date 2026-07-30 is 34 days out, but buyer confirmed Q3 restart (July–September 2026) on 5/26 call, meaning the earliest realistic restart is early July (~2 weeks from today). Original decision timeline (late Feb 2026) slipped to end-Q1, then end-Q2, now Q3—a 3+ month delay. Even if the Q3 restart happens on schedule, the remaining path (recap demo → executive alignment → IT scoping → legal sign-off → commercial negotiation → contract) cannot complete in 34 days. Close date will slip to Q3 at earliest, likely Q4.

Economic Buyer (Jackie Zhang, VP Procurement, or COO above her) has not validated budget, timeline, or strategic fit. Jackie attended only the first two discovery calls (12/3, 12/12) and has not engaged since January 2026 despite multiple rep attempts to loop her back in. COO (final budget approver per 5/26 call) has never been engaged. Technical validation is complete (6 demos, strong Tharun engagement), but without EB sign-off, the deal cannot advance from the relevant stakeholder to Proposal/Negotiation, and the Best Case forecast is indefensible.

No formal ROI model or savings quantification has been delivered. Buyer confirmed ROI and pricing are decision gates (Tharun on 1/29: 'decision depends on total/return on investment'; on 5/26: 'cost also matters... end of the day'), and pricing was shared on 1/30, but no ROI workshop or savings calculation (time savings, compliance-risk reduction, maverick-spend capture) has been provided. The business case is qualitative (confirmed operational pain: $300–400M spend, 9–10 FTEs, manual processes, no contract alerts) but not financial. Buyer is evaluating a phased approach (CLM first, then sourcing, then AI if budget allows per 1/29 call), signaling budget constraint. Budget vulnerability: buyer may defer the full-suite investment if ROI is not proven, retaining the Coupa+Ariba+custom-Power-BI status quo.

D365/Coupa bidirectional integration complexity is a stated decision criterion (12/12: 'integration capability is critical... must sync with D365, Coupa, Power BI') and an unquantified risk. IT lead Ryan Anderson attended three calls (12/12, 1/14, 1/29) for integration questions, but no formal IT deep-dive or scoping call outcome has been documented. On 2/26, Tharun stated the plan is to 'engage IT to assess integration complexity... before finalizing AI scope and budget,' but this has not occurred. Buyer's concern: if the D365/Coupa sync is too complex or costly, it undermines the ROI and could kill the deal. Integration scoping is the second gate (alongside ROI) blocking commercial negotiation.

Legal team (Chris Klein, Laura) holds veto power on CLM vendor with 'equal weightage' in the decision (Tharun on 2/26 call), but no formal legal sign-off or scorecard has been provided post-3/11 CLM demo. Recommended move: secure Jackie + COO executive connect in early July (when buyer is ready per 6/22 rep outreach) to validate budget, re-anchor Q3 timeline, and convert technical validation into commercial momentum. Owner: deal owner's manager (executive connect) to run the call given deal size ($115K) and late Formal Evaluation stage.

No quantified ROI case delivered. value.name: Coupa
value.quote: no plans to replace Dynamics 365 or Coupa, requiring coexistence and data synchronization
value.how_we_win: Position Zycus CLM/sourcing/AI as complementary; demonstrate clean bidirectional Coupa/D365 integration so it is additive, not a rip-and-replace.

value.name: Coupa
value.quote: Not looking to replace existing systems like Dynamics and Coupa, but evaluating external sourcing solutions while emphasizing integration and clear ROI for first technology investment. (Tharun, 12/3/25 discovery call)
value.how_we_win: Coupa is retained for supplier onboarding and invoicing only. Buyer confirmed on 5/26 call: 'I don't believe we'll be getting rid of Coupa... if a tool can help us with AI platforms and analytics, I don't think Coupa is able to meet us in those aspects.' Position Zycus as complementary to Coupa (integrate with it, don't replace it) and focus on CLM + sourcing + AI gaps Coupa cannot fill.

value.name: Coupa
value.quote: some solutions were not as comprehensive as we were expecting... AI platforms and analytics part, Coupa is not able to meet us in those aspects
value.how_we_win: Zycus AI (Merlin negotiation agent Anna, conversational analytics Insight Studio) and unified sourcing suite (iSource, auctions, RFPs, analytics) differentiate vs Coupa's basic CLM. Buyer already validated Coupa is insufficient for AI and analytics; emphasize Merlin/Anna autonomous negotiation + predictive spend analytics as Phase 1 ROI drivers, and position Zycus as the AI-native layer on top of retained Coupa invoicing/onboarding.

value.name: Coupa

value.how_we_win: Position Merlin AI negotiation agent and unified CLM+sourcing+analytics as superior to Coupa's bolt-on modules; quantify ROI to justify switch cost and make AI the Phase 1 wedge.

value.name: SAP Ariba
value.quote: You're using Ariba as a portal wherein you're doing e bidding. The communication just happens between Coupa and d365. When it comes to Ariba, it's completely like email communication with Blackstone. They do the bidding from their side using the Ariba platform, and they share the result through the Excel file to us.
value.how_we_win: Position Zycus iSource as a fully integrated, D365/Coupa-synced sourcing platform (RFPs + auctions + supplier discovery + AI negotiation) vs. Ariba's email-based, Blackstone-intermediated auction silo. Highlight Ariba's lack of integration and limited scope (auctions only, no RFPs, no intake, no AI) as the reason DuBois is evaluating external solutions.

value.name: Do-Nothing / Status Quo
value.quote: We don't have a tool which tracks, okay, a contract is about to expire on a certain date or pushes any alert messages or a tool which tracks how much we have spent right now associated to a certain contract. We're kind of like extracting the reports from the system and linking it to the contracts through the Power BI platform, which is customized by our internal team. When it comes to indirect, we don't have any approved vendor list or something like that. We're going ahead and manually tracking that via email-based RFQs.
value.how_we_win: Quantify the cost of the status quo (9–10 FTEs managing $300–400M spend with manual email RFQs, custom Power BI reports, no contract alerts, no spend-utilization tracking) and prove Zycus delivers measurable time savings, compliance-risk reduction, and savings capture that pays for itself in <18 months. Make inaction the riskiest option by tying the pain to dollars and headcount.
